findings.title: Handler dispatch loop duplicated identically across all 3 channels
findings.description: The handler dispatch loop (iterating over messageHandlers, invoking each, catching sync errors, and attaching .catch to async results) is copied verbatim across email/index.ts (lines 198-208), feishu/index.ts (lines 197-207), and dingtalk/index.ts (lines 204-214). This is 11 lines x 3 = 33 lines of near-identical code. Since this pattern appears 3 times with identical logic, it crosses the threshold for extraction.

findings.title: setStatus method duplicated identically across all 3 channels
findings.description: The setStatus method is identical in all three channel classes (email lines 65-75, feishu lines 69-79, dingtalk lines 67-77). Along with setStatus, the fields _status, statusHandlers, onStatusChange, messageHandlers, and onMessage are also identical across all three. Together these account for ~35 lines of duplicated boilerplate per class (~105 lines total across 3 classes).

findings.title: connect() error-handling pattern duplicated across all 3 channels
findings.description: All three connect() methods follow the identical pattern: setStatus('connecting') -> guard on !this.deps -> try { channel-specific setup; setStatus('connected') } catch { setStatus('error'); throw }. The disconnect() methods follow a similar pattern. The structural skeleton is duplicated even though the inner logic differs. This is less critical than the handler dispatch duplication since only the boilerplate (not the full method) is duplicated.

findings.title: StatusChangeHandler type duplicated across all 3 files
findings.description: The type alias `StatusChangeHandler = (status: ChannelStatus) => void` is independently declared in each of the three channel files (email line 42, feishu line 47, dingtalk line 40). This is a shared contract that belongs in the shared types module.

positive_observations: All three channel classes are well-structured with consistent API surface matching the Channel interface
Guard clauses used effectively (early return in setupExistsListener, handleIncomingMessage bot-filter)
Error handling is thorough - connect/disconnect wrap in try/catch with proper status transitions and cause chaining
Function lengths are reasonable: no function exceeds 35 lines
Maximum nesting depth is 3, which is within acceptable thresholds
Dependency injection via constructor deps parameter enables testability without mocks
Structured logging used consistently with contextual fields (no console.log)
